(**GDP**) is a [monetary](/wiki/Money "Money") [measure](/wiki/Measurement_in_economics "Measurement in economics") of the total [market value](/wiki/Market_value "Market value")[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) of all of the [final goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good") and services which are produced and rendered during a specific period of time (usually a year) by a [country](/wiki/Country "Country")[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) or countries.[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) GDP is often used to measure the economic activity of a country or region.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) The major components of GDP are consumption, government spending, net exports (exports minus imports), and investment. Changing any of these factors can increase the size of the economy. For example, population growth through mass immigration can raise consumption and demand for public services, thereby contributing to GDP growth. However, GDP is not a measure of overall [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") or well-being, as it does not account for how income is distributed among the population. A country may rank high in GDP but still experience [jobless growth](/wiki/Jobless_growth "Jobless growth") depending on its planned economic structure and strategies. Dividing total GDP by the population gives an idealized rough measure of [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) Several national and international economic organizations, such as the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") and the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), maintain their own definitions of GDP.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

**Gross domestic product** (**GDP**) is a [monetary](/wiki/Money "Money") [measure](/wiki/Measurement_in_economics "Measurement in economics") of the total [market value](/wiki/Market_value "Market value")[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) of all of the [final goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good") and services which are produced and rendered during a specific period of time (usually a year) by a [country](/wiki/Country "Country")[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) or countries.[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) GDP is often used to measure the economic activity of a country or region.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) The major components of GDP are consumption, government spending, net exports (exports minus imports), and investment. Changing any of these factors can increase the size of the economy. For example, population growth through mass immigration can raise consumption and demand for public services, thereby contributing to GDP growth. However, GDP is not a measure of overall [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") or well-being, as it does not account for how income is distributed among the population. A country may rank high in GDP but still experience [jobless growth](/wiki/Jobless_growth "Jobless growth") depending on its planned economic structure and strategies. Dividing total GDP by the population gives an idealized rough measure of [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[3]](#cite_note-:4-3) Several national and international economic organizations, such as the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") and the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), maintain their own definitions of GDP.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

GDP is often used as a metric for [international comparisons](/wiki/International_comparisons "International comparisons") as well as a [broad measure of economic progress](/wiki/Broad_measures_of_economic_progress "Broad measures of economic progress"). It serves as a statistical indicator of national development and progress. Total GDP can also be broken down into the contribution of each industry or sector of the economy.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Nominal GDP is useful when comparing national economies on the international market using current [exchange rate](/wiki/Exchange_rate "Exchange rate").[[9]](#cite_note-9) To compare economies over time [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") can be adjusted by comparing [real instead of nominal values](/wiki/Real_and_nominal_value "Real and nominal value"). For cross-country comparisons, GDP figures are often adjusted for differences in the [cost of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living") using [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP). [GDP per capita at purchasing power parity](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") can be useful for comparing [living standards](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") between nations.

GDP has been criticized for leaving out key [externalities](/wiki/Externality "Externality"), such as [resource extraction](/wiki/Resource_extraction "Resource extraction"),[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] [environmental impact](/wiki/Environmental_impact "Environmental impact") and [unpaid domestic work](/wiki/Unpaid_work "Unpaid work").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Alternative economic indicators such as [doughnut economics](/wiki/Doughnut_(economic_model) "Doughnut (economic model)") use other measures, such as the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") or [Better Life Index](/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index "OECD Better Life Index"), as better approaches to measuring the effect of the economy on [human development](/wiki/Human_development_(economics) "Human development (economics)") and [well-being](/wiki/Well-being "Well-being").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Quarterly_gross_domestic_product.png/330px-Quarterly_gross_domestic_product.png)](/wiki/File:Quarterly_gross_domestic_product.png)

[U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") [YoY](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/year-over-year "wikt:year-over-year") Quarterly gross domestic product growth rate

Sir [William Petty](/wiki/William_Petty "William Petty") came up with a concept of GDP, to calculate the [tax burden](/wiki/Tax_burden "Tax burden"), and argue landlords were unfairly taxed during [warfare between the Dutch and the English](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_Wars "Anglo-Dutch Wars") between 1652 and 1674.[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Charles Davenant](/wiki/Charles_Davenant "Charles Davenant") developed the method further in 1695.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The modern concept of GDP was first developed by [Simon Kuznets](/wiki/Simon_Kuznets "Simon Kuznets") for a 1934 [U.S. Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") report, where he warned against its use as a measure of welfare (see below under *[limitations and criticisms](#Limitations_and_criticisms)*).[[13]](#cite_note-kuznets-13) After the [Bretton Woods Conference](/wiki/Bretton_Woods_Conference "Bretton Woods Conference") in 1944, GDP became the main tool for measuring a country's economy.[[14]](#cite_note-14) At that time [gross national product](/wiki/Gross_national_product "Gross national product") (GNP) was the preferred estimate, which differed from GDP in that it measured production by a country's citizens at home and abroad rather than its "resident institutional units" (see [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") definition above). The switch from GNP to GDP in the United States occurred in 1991. The role that measurements of GDP played in World War II was crucial to the subsequent political acceptance of GDP values as indicators of national development and progress.[[15]](#cite_note-15) A crucial role was played here by the [U.S. Department of Commerce](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Commerce "U.S. Department of Commerce") under [Milton Gilbert](/wiki/Milton_Gilbert "Milton Gilbert") where ideas from Kuznets were embedded into [institutions](/wiki/Institution "Institution").

The history of the concept of GDP should be distinguished from the history of changes in many ways of estimating it. The value added by firms is relatively easy to calculate from their accounts, but the value added by the [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector"), by financial industries, and by [intangible asset](/wiki/Intangible_asset "Intangible asset") creation is more complex. These activities are increasingly important in developed economies, and the international conventions governing their estimation and their inclusion or exclusion in GDP regularly change in an attempt to keep up with industrial advances. In the words of one academic economist, "The actual number for GDP is, therefore, the product of a vast patchwork of statistics and a complicated set of processes carried out on the raw data to fit them to the conceptual framework."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[China](/wiki/China "China") officially adopted GDP in 1993 as its indicator of economic performance. Previously, China had relied on a Marxist-inspired national accounting system.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Determining gross domestic product (GDP)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Determining gross domestic product (GDP)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/How_is_GDP_calculated%3F.png/330px-How_is_GDP_calculated%3F.png)](/wiki/File:How_is_GDP_calculated%3F.png)

An infographic explaining how GDP is calculated in the UK

GDP can be determined in three ways, all of which should, theoretically, give the same result. They are the production (or output or value added) approach, the income approach, and the speculated expenditure approach. It is representative of the total output and income within an economy.

The most direct of the three is the production approach, which sums the outputs of every class of enterprise to arrive at the total. The expenditure approach works on the principle that all of the products must be bought by somebody, therefore the value of the total product must be equal to people's total expenditures in buying things. The income approach works on the principle that the incomes of the productive factors ("producers", colloquially) must be equal to the value of their product, and determines GDP by finding the sum of all producers' incomes.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Production approach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production approach")]

Also known as the Value Added Approach, it calculates how much value is contributed at each stage of production.

This approach mirrors the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") (Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development) definition given above.

1. Estimate the gross value of domestic output out of the many various economic activities;
2. Determine the [intermediate consumption](/wiki/Intermediate_consumption "Intermediate consumption"), i.e., the cost of material, supplies and services used to produce final goods or services.
3. Deduct intermediate consumption from gross value to obtain the gross value added.

[Gross value added](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added") = gross value of output – value of intermediate consumption.

Value of output = value of the total sales of goods and services plus the value of changes in the inventory.

The sum of the gross value added in the various economic activities is known as "GDP at factor cost".

GDP at factor cost plus indirect taxes less subsidies on products = "GDP at producer price".

For measuring the output of domestic product, economic activities (i.e. industries) are classified into various sectors. After classifying economic activities, the output of each sector is calculated by any of the following two methods:

1. By multiplying the output of each sector by their respective market price and adding them together
2. By collecting data on gross sales and inventories from the records of companies and adding them together

The value of output of all sectors is then added to get the gross value of output at factor cost. Subtracting each sector's intermediate consumption from gross output value gives the GVA (=GDP) at factor cost. Adding indirect tax minus subsidies to GVA (GDP) at factor cost gives the "GVA (GDP) at producer prices".

### Income approach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Income approach")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/U.S._GDP_Income_Basis.png/500px-U.S._GDP_Income_Basis.png)](/wiki/File:U.S._GDP_Income_Basis.png)

U.S 2015 GDP computed on the income basis

The second way of estimating GDP is to use "the sum of primary incomes distributed by resident producer units".[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)

If GDP is calculated this way it is sometimes called gross domestic income (GDI), or GDP (I). GDI should provide the same amount as the expenditure method described later. By definition, GDI is equal to GDP. In practice, however, measurement errors will make the two figures slightly off when reported by national statistical agencies.

This method measures GDP by adding incomes that firms pay households for factors of production they hire – wages for labour, interest for capital, rent for land and profits for entrepreneurship.

The US "National Income and Product Accounts" divide incomes into five categories:

1. Wages, salaries, and supplementary labor income
2. Corporate profits
3. Interest and miscellaneous investment income
4. Income earned by sole proprietors and from the Housing subsector (net of expenses)
5. Net income from transfer payments from businesses

These five income components sum to net domestic income at factor cost.

Two adjustments must be made to get GDP:

1. Taxes on production and imports minus subsidies are added to get from factor cost to market prices.
2. Depreciation (or [capital consumption allowance](/wiki/Capital_consumption_allowance "Capital consumption allowance")) is added to get from net domestic product to gross domestic product.

Total income can be subdivided according to various schemes, leading to various formulae for GDP measured by the income approach. A common one is:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

:   **GDP** = Compensation of employees**COE** + [gross operating surplus](/wiki/Gross_operating_surplus "Gross operating surplus")**GOS** + [gross mixed income](/wiki/Gross_mixed_income "Gross mixed income")**GMI** +  taxes less subsidies on production and imports**TP&M** − **SP&M**

* **Compensation of employees** (**COE**) measures the total remuneration to employees for work done. It includes wages and salaries, as well as employer contributions to [social security](/wiki/Social_security "Social security") and other such programs.
* **Gross operating surplus** (**GOS**) is the surplus due to owners of incorporated businesses. Often called [profits](/wiki/Profit_(accounting) "Profit (accounting)"), although only a subset of total costs are subtracted from [gross output](/wiki/Gross_output "Gross output") to calculate GOS.
* **Gross mixed income** (**GMI**) is the same measure as GOS, but for unincorporated businesses. This often includes most small businesses.

The sum of **COE**, **GOS** and **GMI** is called total factor income; it is the income of all of the factors of production in society. It measures the value of GDP at factor (basic) prices. The difference between basic prices and final prices (those used in the expenditure calculation) is the total taxes and subsidies that the government has levied or paid on that production. So adding taxes less subsidies on production and imports converts GDP(I) at [factor cost](/wiki/Factor_cost "Factor cost") to GDP(I) at final prices.

Total factor income is also sometimes expressed as:

:   *Total factor income* = *employee compensation* + *corporate profits* + *proprietor's income* + *rental income* + *net interest*[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Expenditure approach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Expenditure approach")]

The third way to estimate GDP is to calculate the sum of the final uses of goods and services (all uses except intermediate consumption) measured in purchasers' prices.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)

Market goods that are produced are purchased by someone. In the case where a good is produced and unsold, the standard accounting convention is that the producer has bought the good from themselves. Therefore, measuring the total expenditure used to buy things is a way of measuring production. This is known as the expenditure method of calculating GDP.

#### Components of GDP by expenditure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Components of GDP by expenditure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/GDP_Categories_-_United_States.png/500px-GDP_Categories_-_United_States.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_Categories_-_United_States.png)

U.S. GDP computed on the expenditure basis

**GDP (Y)** is the sum of **consumption (C)**, **investment (I)**, **government expenditures (G)** and **net exports (X − M)**.

:   **Y** = **C** + **I** + **G** + **(X − M)**

Here is a description of each GDP component:

* **C (consumption)** is normally the largest GDP component in the economy, consisting of private expenditures in the economy ([household final consumption expenditure](/wiki/Household_final_consumption_expenditure "Household final consumption expenditure")). These personal expenditures fall under one of the following categories: [durable goods](/wiki/Durable_good "Durable good"), nondurable goods, and services. Examples include food, rent, jewelry, gasoline, and medical expenses, but not the purchase of new housing.
* **I (investment)** includes, for instance, business investment in equipment, but does not include exchanges of existing assets. Examples include the construction of a new [mine](/wiki/Mining "Mining"), the purchase of software, or the purchase of machinery and equipment for a factory. Spending by households (not the government) on new houses is also included in investment. In contrast to its colloquial meaning, "investment" in GDP does not mean purchases related to financial investments. Buying [financial products](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services") is classed as '[saving](/wiki/Saving "Saving")', as opposed to investment. This avoids double-counting: if one buys shares in a company, and the company uses the money received to build a plant, purchase equipment, etc., the amount will be counted toward GDP when the company spends the money on those things; to also count it when one gives it to the company would be to count two times an amount that only corresponds to one group of products. Buying [bonds](/wiki/Bond_(finance) "Bond (finance)") or companies' [equity shares](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") is a swapping of [deeds](/wiki/Deed "Deed"), a transfer of claims on future production, not directly an expenditure on products; buying an existing building will involve a positive investment by the buyer and a negative investment by the seller, netting to zero overall investment.
* **G (government spending)** is the sum of [government expenditures](/wiki/Government_spending "Government spending") on [final goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good") and services. It includes salaries of [public servants](/wiki/Public_servants "Public servants"), purchases of weapons for the military and any investment expenditure by a government. It does not include any [transfer payments](/wiki/Transfer_payment "Transfer payment"), such as [social security](/wiki/Social_security "Social security") or [unemployment benefits](/wiki/Unemployment_benefits "Unemployment benefits"). Analyses outside the US will often treat government investment as part of *investment* rather than *government spending*.
* **X (exports)** represents gross exports. GDP captures the amount a country produces, including goods and services produced for other nations' consumption, therefore exports are added.
* **M (imports)** represents gross imports. Imports are subtracted since imported goods will be included in the terms **C**, **I**, and **G**, and must be deducted to avoid counting foreign [supply](/wiki/Supply_and_demand "Supply and demand") as domestic.

**C**, **I**, and **G** are expenditures on [final goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good") and services; expenditures on intermediate goods and services do not count. (Intermediate goods and services are those used by businesses to produce other goods and services within the accounting year.[[20]](#cite_note-20)) So for example if a [car manufacturer](/wiki/Car_manufacturer "Car manufacturer") buys [auto parts](/wiki/Auto_parts "Auto parts"), assembles the car and sells it, only the final car sold is counted towards the GDP. Meanwhile, if a person buys replacement auto parts to install them on their car, those are counted towards the GDP.

According to the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis, which is responsible for calculating the national accounts in the United States, "In general, the source data for the expenditures components are considered more reliable than those for the income components [see income method, above]."[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Encyclopedia Britannica records an alternate way of measuring exports minus imports: notating it as the single variable NX.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Nominal GDP and real GDP

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Nominal GDP and real GDP")]

The raw GDP figure given by the equations above is called the nominal, historical, or current GDP. When comparing GDP figures from one year to another, compensating for changes in the value of money—for the effects of inflation or deflation—is desirable. To make it more meaningful for year-to-year comparisons, a nominal GDP may be multiplied by the ratio between the value of money in the year the GDP was measured and the value of money in a base year.

For example, suppose a country's GDP in 1990 was $100 million and its GDP in 2000 was $300 million. Suppose also that inflation had halved the value of its currency over that period. To meaningfully compare its GDP in 2000 to its GDP in 1990, we could multiply the GDP in 2000 by one-half, to make it relative to 1990 as a base year. The result would be that the GDP in 2000 equals $300 million × 1⁄2 = $150 million, *in 1990 monetary terms.* We would see that the country's GDP had realistically increased 50 [percent](/wiki/Percentage#Percentage_increase_and_decrease "Percentage") over that period, not 200 percent, as it might appear from the raw GDP data. The GDP adjusted for changes in money value in this way is called the [real GDP](/wiki/Real_gross_domestic_product "Real gross domestic product").

The factor used to convert GDP from current to constant values in this way is called the *[GDP deflator](/wiki/GDP_deflator "GDP deflator")*. Unlike [consumer price index](/wiki/Consumer_price_index "Consumer price index"), which measures inflation or deflation in the price of household consumer goods, the GDP deflator measures changes in the prices of all domestically produced goods and services in an economy including investment goods and government services, as well as household consumption goods.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### National measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: National measurement")]

Main article: [National agencies responsible for GDP measurement](/wiki/National_agencies_responsible_for_GDP_measurement "National agencies responsible for GDP measurement")

Within each country GDP is normally measured by a national government statistical agency, as [private sector](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") organizations normally do not have access to the information required (especially information on expenditure and production by governments).

#### International standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: International standards")]

The international standard for measuring GDP is contained in the book *[System of National Accounts](/wiki/United_Nations_System_of_National_Accounts_(UNSNA) "United Nations System of National Accounts (UNSNA)")* (2008), which was prepared by representatives of the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") and [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"). The publication is normally referred to as SNA2008 to distinguish it from the previous edition published in 1993 (SNA93) or 1968 (called SNA68)[[24]](#cite_note-Central_Bureau_of_Statistics-24)

SNA2008 provides a set of rules and procedures for the measurement of national accounts. The standards are designed to be flexible, to allow for differences in local statistical needs and conditions.

### Problems with GDP data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Problems with GDP data")]

See also: [Economic data](/wiki/Economic_data "Economic data")

A peer-reviewed study published in the *[Journal of Political Economy](/wiki/Journal_of_Political_Economy "Journal of Political Economy")* in October 2022 found signs of manipulation of [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") statistics in the majority of countries.[[25]](#cite_note-:6-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) This manipulation was greatest in countries that were semi-authoritarian/authoritarian, or did not have effective [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers"). The study took the annual growth in the brightness of lights at night, as measured by satellites, and compared it to officially reported economic growth. Authoritarian states consistently reported higher growth in GDP than the growth in night lights would suggest, an effect that could not be explained by economic structures, sector composition or other factors.[[25]](#cite_note-:6-25)

[Corporate havens](/wiki/Corporate_haven "Corporate haven") can also have a [distorted GDP](/wiki/Corporate_haven#Distorted_GDP/GNP "Corporate haven").

## Relation to gross national income

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Relation to gross national income")]

GDP can be contrasted with the [gross national income](/wiki/Gross_national_income "Gross national income") (GNI) also known as gross national product (GNP). The difference is that GDP defines its scope according to location, while GNI defines its scope according to ownership. In a global context, [world GDP and world GNI](/wiki/Gross_world_product "Gross world product") are, therefore, equivalent terms.

GDP is a product produced within a country's borders; GNI is product produced by enterprises owned by a country's citizens. The two would be the same if all of the productive enterprises in a country were owned by its own citizens and those citizens did not own productive enterprises in any other countries. In practice, however, foreign ownership makes GDP and GNI non-identical. Production within a country's borders, but by an enterprise owned by somebody outside the country, counts as part of its GDP but not its GNI; on the other hand, production by an enterprise located outside the country, but owned by one of its citizens, counts as part of its GNI but not its GDP.
For example, the GNI of the [US](/wiki/United_States "United States") is the value of output produced by American-owned firms, regardless of where the firms are located.

Gross national income (GNI) equals GDP plus income receipts from the rest of the world minus income payments to the rest of the world.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 1991, the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") switched from using GNP to using GDP as its primary measure of production.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The relationship between United States GDP and GNP is shown in table 1.7.5 of the *[National Income and Product Accounts](/wiki/National_Income_and_Product_Accounts "National Income and Product Accounts")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Other examples that amplify differences between GDP and GNI can be found by comparing indicators of developed and developing countries. The GDP of Japan for 2020 was 5.05559 trillion.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Predictably, as a developed country, Japan has a higher GNI of 5.16915 trillion for the same year,[[32]](#cite_note-32) an increase of 113.560 million. This is indicative of the production level in the country being higher than that of national production. On the other hand, the case with [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia") is the opposite with its GNI in 2023 being lower than its GDP by 3.85 billion. This shows countries that receive investments and foreign aid from abroad.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Limitations and criticisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Limitations and criticisms")]

GDP does not include several factors that influence the standard of living. In particular, it fails to account for:

* **[Externalities](/wiki/Externalities "Externalities")** – Economic growth may entail an increase in negative externalities that are not directly measured in GDP.[[35]](#cite_note-soL-35)[[36]](#cite_note-limitations-36) Increased industrial output might grow GDP, but any pollution is not counted.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* **Non-market transactions** – GDP excludes activities that are not provided through the market, such as household production, bartering of goods and services, and volunteer or unpaid services.
* **Non-monetary economy** – GDP omits economies where no money comes into play at all, resulting in inaccurate or abnormally low GDP figures. For example, in countries with major business transactions occurring informally, portions of local economy are not easily registered. [Bartering](/wiki/Barter_(economics) "Barter (economics)") may be more prominent than the use of money, even extending to services.[[36]](#cite_note-limitations-36)
* **Quality improvements and inclusion of new products** – by not fully adjusting for quality improvements and new products, GDP understates true [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth"). For instance, although computers today are less expensive and more powerful than computers from the past, GDP treats them as the same products by only accounting for their monetary value. The introduction of new products is also difficult to measure accurately and is not reflected in GDP although it may increase the standard of living. For example, even the richest person in 1900 could not purchase standard products, such as antibiotics and cell phones, that an average consumer can buy today, since such modern conveniences did not exist then.
* **[Broken window fallacy](/wiki/Broken_window_fallacy "Broken window fallacy")** - The Broken window fallacy is an economic phenomenon stating that GDP will treat money spent on repairing damages even though there isn’t a net benefit to society.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In other words GDP doesn’t account for the fact that there was destruction in the first place. For example if there is a natural disaster and the government spends money on repairing housing, the cost of repairing housing will be included in the government spending portion on GDP even though people might have been equally well off if there wasn’t government spending and a natural disaster.

[Simon Kuznets](/wiki/Simon_Kuznets "Simon Kuznets"), the economist who developed the first comprehensive set of measures of national income, stated in his second report to the U.S. Congress in 1937, in a section titled "Uses and Abuses of National Income Measurements":[[13]](#cite_note-kuznets-13)

> The valuable capacity of the human mind to simplify a complex situation in a compact characterization becomes dangerous when not controlled in terms of definitely stated criteria. With quantitative measurements especially, the definiteness of the result suggests, often misleadingly, a precision and simplicity in the outlines of the object measured. Measurements of national income are subject to this type of illusion and resulting abuse, especially since they deal with matters that are the center of conflict of opposing social groups where the effectiveness of an argument is often contingent upon oversimplification. [...]
> All these qualifications upon estimates of national income as an index of productivity are just as important when income measurements are interpreted from the point of view of economic welfare. But in the latter case additional difficulties will be suggested to anyone who wants to penetrate below the surface of total figures and market values. Economic welfare cannot be adequately measured unless the personal distribution of income is known. And no income measurement undertakes to estimate the reverse side of income, that is, the intensity and unpleasantness of effort going into the earning of income. The welfare of a nation can, therefore, scarcely be inferred from a measurement of national income as defined above.

In 1962, Kuznets stated:[[39]](#cite_note-39)

> Distinctions must be kept in mind between quantity and quality of growth, between costs and returns, and between the short and long run. Goals for more growth should specify more growth of what and for what.

GDP as initially defined includes spending on goods and services that would shrink if underlying problems were solved or reduced - for example, medical care, crime-fighting, and the military. During World War II, Kuznets came to argue that military spending should be excluded during peacetime. This idea did not become popular; these activities are tracked because they fit into macroeconomic models (e.g. military spending uses up capital and labor).[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Ever since the development of GDP, multiple observers have pointed out limitations of using GDP as the overarching measure of economic and social progress. Furthermore, the GDP does not consider human health nor the educational aspect of a population.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Instances of GDP measures have been considered numbers that are artificial constructs.[[42]](#cite_note-42) American politician [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy")[[43]](#cite_note-43) criticized GDP (or GNI), listing many examples of bad things it does count and good things it does not count:

> Gross National Product counts air pollution and cigarette advertising, and ambulances to clear our highways of carnage. It counts special locks for our doors and the jails for the people who break them. It counts the destruction of the redwood and the loss of our natural wonder in chaotic sprawl. It counts napalm and counts nuclear warheads and armored cars for the police to fight the riots in our cities. It counts Whitman's rifle and Speck's knife, and the television programs which glorify violence in order to sell toys to our children. Yet the gross national product does not allow for the health of our children, the quality of their education or the joy of their play. It does not include the beauty of our poetry or the strength of our marriages, the intelligence of our public debate or the integrity of our public officials. It measures neither our wit nor our courage, neither our wisdom nor our learning, neither our compassion nor our devotion to our country, it measures everything in short, except that which makes life worthwhile. And it can tell us everything about America except why we are proud that we are Americans.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Deficit spending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Deficit spending")]

[Deficit spending](/wiki/Deficit_spending "Deficit spending") increases GDP in case of positive [fiscal multipliers](/wiki/Fiscal_multiplier "Fiscal multiplier").[[45]](#cite_note-45) GDP as a metric can incentivize politicians to [overspend](/wiki/Overspending "Overspending").[[46]](#cite_note-46) Counterfactual GDP under a [balanced budget](/wiki/Balanced_budget "Balanced budget") scenario can be estimated.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

If a country becomes increasingly in debt, and spends large amounts of income on debt [interest expense](/wiki/Interest_expense "Interest expense"), will be reflected in a decreased GNI[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but not a decreased GDP. Similarly, if a country sells off its resources to entities outside their country this will also be reflected over time in decreased GNI, but not decreased GDP. This would make the use of GDP more attractive for politicians in countries with increasing national debt and decreasing assets.

### Unpaid work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Unpaid work")]

GDP excludes the value of household and other [unpaid work](/wiki/Unpaid_work "Unpaid work"). Some, including [Martha Nussbaum](/wiki/Martha_Nussbaum "Martha Nussbaum"), argue that this value should be included in measuring GDP, as household labor is largely a [substitute](/wiki/Substitute_good "Substitute good") for goods and services that would otherwise be purchased with money.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Even under conservative estimates, the value of unpaid labor in Australia has been calculated to be over 50% of the country's GDP.[[49]](#cite_note-49) A later study analyzed this value in other countries, with results ranging from a low of about 15% in Canada (using conservative estimates) to high of nearly 70% in the United Kingdom (using more liberal estimates). For the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the value was estimated to be between about 20% on the low end to nearly 50% on the high end, depending on the methodology being used.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Because many public policies are shaped by GDP calculations and by the related field of [national accounts](/wiki/National_accounts "National accounts"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) public policy might differ if unpaid work were included in total GDP. Some economists have advocated for changes in the way public policies are formed and implemented.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Some have pointed out that GDP did not adapt to sociotechnical changes to give a more accurate picture of the modern economy and does not encapsulate the [value](/wiki/Value_(economics) "Value (economics)") of new activities such as delivering price-free information and entertainment on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-:5-54) In 2017 [Diane Coyle](/wiki/Diane_Coyle "Diane Coyle") explained that GDP excludes much unpaid work, writing that "many people contribute free digital [work](/wiki/Work_(human_activity) "Work (human activity)") such as writing [open-source software](/wiki/FOSS "FOSS") that can substitute for marketed equivalents, and it clearly has great economic value despite a price of zero", which constitutes a common criticism "of the reliance on GDP as the measure of economic success" especially after the emergence of the [digital economy](/wiki/Digital_economy "Digital economy").[[55]](#cite_note-55) A 2025 study in the *American Economic Journal* devised a new GDP measurement (GDP-B) that accounts for the welfare value of new goods and free goods.[[54]](#cite_note-:5-54)

In 2019, Erik Brynjolfsson and Avinash Collis argued that GDP does not reflect the growing value of many digital goods because they have zero price.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Along with several coauthors, they proposed an alternative approach, [GDP-B](https://digitaleconomy.stanford.edu/research/gdp-b-a-new-well-being-metric-in-the-era-of-the-digital-economy/), which is based on measuring the *benefits* of goods and services, rather than their price or cost.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Health")]

In 2013 scientists reported that large [improvements](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") in [health](/wiki/Health "Health") only lead to modest long-term increases in GDP per capita.[[58]](#cite_note-58) After developing an abstract metric similar to GDP, the [Center for Partnership Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Partnership_Studies "Center for Partnership Studies") highlighted that GDP "and other metrics that reflect and perpetuate them" may not be useful for facilitating the production of products and provision of services that are useful – or comparatively more useful – to society, and instead may "actually encourage, rather than discourage, destructive activities".[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) The number of [obese](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity") adults was approximately 600 million (12%) in 2015.[[61]](#cite_note-NEJM2017-61)[*[*non sequitur*](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Environment")]

See also: [Eco-economic decoupling](/wiki/Eco-economic_decoupling "Eco-economic decoupling")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Absolute-decoupling-Growth-and-falling-emissions-all.png/500px-Absolute-decoupling-Growth-and-falling-emissions-all.png)](/wiki/File:Absolute-decoupling-Growth-and-falling-emissions-all.png)

Eco-economic decoupling between GDP growth and [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") decrease

Many [environmentalists](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism") argue that GDP is a poor measure of social progress because it does not take into account harm to the [environment](/wiki/Biosphere "Biosphere").[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In the language of economics, everything comes down to its monetary value.[[64]](#cite_note-:3-64) In essence, GDP rewards behaviors that are detrimental to the environment.[[64]](#cite_note-:3-64) GDP also does not capture certain phenomena impacting citizens' well-being.[[65]](#cite_note-65) For example, traffic jams could cause GDP to increase as there is a higher consumption of gasoline, however, GDP fails to consider citizens' well-being in terms of the quality of air due to air pollution from the traffic jams.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Various alternatives have been developed(see below).

A 2020 study found that "poor regions' GDP grows faster by attracting more polluting [production](/wiki/Production_(economics) "Production (economics)") after connection to China's expressway system.[[67]](#cite_note-67) GDP may not be a tool capable of recognizing how much [natural capital](/wiki/Natural_capital "Natural capital") agents of the economy are building or protecting.[[68]](#cite_note-68)
In 2020 scientists, as part of a [World Scientists' Warning to Humanity](/wiki/World_Scientists%27_Warning_to_Humanity "World Scientists' Warning to Humanity")-associated series, warned that worldwide growth in [affluence](/wiki/Affluence "Affluence") in terms of GDP-metrics has increased [resource use](/wiki/Resource_use "Resource use") and [pollutant emissions](/wiki/Pollutant "Pollutant") with affluent [citizens of the world](/wiki/Global_citizen "Global citizen") – in terms of e.g. resource-intensive consumption – being responsible for most negative environmental impacts and central to a transition to safer, [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainability "Sustainability") conditions. They summarised evidence, presented solution approaches and stated that far-reaching [lifestyle](/wiki/Lifestyle_(sociology) "Lifestyle (sociology)") [changes](/wiki/Behavioural_change_theories "Behavioural change theories") need to [complement technological advancements](/wiki/Technology_and_society "Technology and society") and that existing societies, economies and cultures [incite](/wiki/Incentive "Incentive") [consumption expansion](/wiki/Overconsumption "Overconsumption") and that the [structural imperative](/wiki/Structural_change "Structural change") for [growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") in [competitive](/wiki/Competition_(economics) "Competition (economics)") [market economies](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") inhibits [societal change](/wiki/Societal_change "Societal change").[[69]](#cite_note-phys-affluence-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-gdp-affluence-71) Sarah Arnold, Senior Economist at the [New Economics Foundation](/wiki/New_Economics_Foundation "New Economics Foundation") (NEF) stated that "GDP includes activities that are detrimental to our [economy](/wiki/Economy "Economy") and [society](/wiki/Society "Society") in the long term, such as [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation"), strip mining, overfishing and so on".[[72]](#cite_note-72) The number of trees that are net lost annually is estimated to be approximately 10 billion.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) The global average annual deforested land in the 2015–2020 demi-decade was 10 million hectares and the average annual net forest area loss in the 2000–2010 decade 4.7 million hectares, according to the [Global Forest Resources Assessment 2020](/wiki/Global_Forest_Resources_Assessment_(FRA) "Global Forest Resources Assessment (FRA)").[[75]](#cite_note-75) According to one study, depending on the level of [wealth inequality](/wiki/Wealth_inequality "Wealth inequality"), higher GDP-growth can be associated with more deforestation.[[76]](#cite_note-76) In 2019 "agriculture and [agribusiness](/wiki/Agribusiness "Agribusiness")" accounted for 24% of the GDP of Brazil, [where a large share of annual net tropical forest loss occurred and is associated with sizable portions of this economic activity domain](/wiki/2019_Amazon_rainforest_wildfires "2019 Amazon rainforest wildfires").[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[Steve Cohen](/wiki/Steven_A._Cohen_(academic) "Steven A. Cohen (academic)") of the Earth Institute elucidated that while GDP does not distinguish between different activities (or [lifestyles](/wiki/Lifestyle_(sociology) "Lifestyle (sociology)")), "all consumption behaviors are not created equal and do not have the same impact on [environmental sustainability](/wiki/Environmental_sustainability "Environmental sustainability")".[[78]](#cite_note-78) [Johan Rockström](/wiki/Johan_Rockstr%C3%B6m "Johan Rockström"), director of the Potsdam Institute for Climate Impact Research, noted that "it's difficult to see if the current G.D.P.-based [model](/wiki/Economic_model "Economic model") of economic growth can go hand-in-hand with rapid [cutting of emissions](/wiki/Mitigation_of_climate_change "Mitigation of climate change")", which nations have agreed to attempt under the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement") in order to mitigate real-world impacts of climate change.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In 1989, [John B. Cobb](/wiki/John_B._Cobb "John B. Cobb") and [Herman Daly](/wiki/Herman_Daly "Herman Daly") introduced [Index of Sustainable Economic Welfare](/wiki/Index_of_Sustainable_Economic_Welfare "Index of Sustainable Economic Welfare") (ISEW) by taking into account other factors such as consumption of nonrenewable resources and degradation of the environment. ISEW is roughly defined as: personal consumption + public non-defensive expenditures − private defensive expenditures + capital formation + services from domestic labour − costs of [environmental degradation](/wiki/Environmental_degradation "Environmental degradation") − depreciation of natural capital.
In 2005, [Med Jones](/wiki/Med_Jones "Med Jones"), an American Economist, at the International Institute of Management, introduced the first secular Gross National Happiness Index a.k.a. [Gross National Well-being](/wiki/Gross_National_Well-being "Gross National Well-being") framework and Index to complement GDP economics with additional seven dimensions, including environment, education, and government, work, social and health (mental and physical) indicators. The proposal was inspired by the King of Bhutan's [GNH](/wiki/Gross_National_Happiness "Gross National Happiness") philosophy.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)
In 2019, Serge Pierre Besanger published a "GDP 3.0" proposal which combines an expanded GNI formula which he calls GNIX, with a [Palma ratio](/wiki/Palma_ratio "Palma ratio") and a set of environmental metrics based on the Daly Rule.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The UK's [Natural Capital Committee](/wiki/Natural_Capital_Committee "Natural Capital Committee") highlighted the shortcomings of GDP in its advice to the [UK](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") Government in 2013, pointing out that GDP "focuses on flows, not stocks. As a result, an economy can run down its assets yet, at the same time, record high levels of GDP growth, until a point is reached where the depleted assets act as a check on future growth". They then went on to say that "it is apparent that the recorded GDP growth rate overstates the [sustainable growth](/wiki/Sustainable_growth "Sustainable growth") rate. Broader measures of wellbeing and wealth are needed for this and there is a danger that short-term decisions based solely on what is currently measured by national accounts may prove to be costly in the long-term".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

China launched the [Gross Ecosystem Product (GEP)](/wiki/Gross_Ecosystem_Product_(GEP) "Gross Ecosystem Product (GEP)") in 2020. It measures the contribution of ecosystems to the economy, including by regulating climate. It spread widely across the country. The first province to issue local rules about GEP was [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang") , and a year later it has already decided the fate of a project in the [Deqing](/wiki/Deqing_County,_Zhejiang "Deqing County, Zhejiang") region. For example, the GEP of Chengtian Radon Spring Nature Reserve has been calculated as US$43 million.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### GDP per capita

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: GDP per capita")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Map_of_countries_by_GDP_%28PPP%29_per_capita_in_2024.svg/500px-Map_of_countries_by_GDP_%28PPP%29_per_capita_in_2024.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita_in_2024.svg)

[Countries by GDP (PPP) per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") in 2024

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| > $60,000  $50,000–$60,000  $40,000–$50,000  $30,000–$40,000 | $20,000–$30,000  $10,000–$20,000  $5,000–$10,000  $2,500–$5,000 | $1,000–$2,500  < $1,000  No data |

GDP changes with [population change](/wiki/Population_change "Population change"). GDP adjusted for population is called *Per-capita GDP* or *GDP per person*. This measures the average production of a person in the country. The major advantage of GDP per capita as an indicator of the standard of living is that it is measured frequently, widely, and consistently. It is measured frequently in that most countries provide information on GDP every quarter, allowing trends to be seen quickly. It is measured widely in that some measure of GDP is available for almost every country in the world, allowing inter-country comparisons. It is measured consistently in that the technical definition of GDP is relatively consistent among countries.

It can be argued that GDP per capita is an indicator of standard of living.[[85]](#cite_note-HDWMSL-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) As a result, GDP per capita as a standard of living is a continued usage because most people have a fairly accurate idea of what it is and know it is tough to come up with quantitative measures for such constructs as happiness, quality of life, and well-being.[[85]](#cite_note-HDWMSL-85) From the perspective of environmental, social and governance (ESG) measures, GDP per capita trends can be influenced by factors such as gender parity and elements of regulatory quality. The change in number of MSMEs (Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises) in the Philippines from 2008 through 2021 would be an example of elements such as the per capita gross domestic product and unemployment rate having significant effect on a developing country with mixed economy.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

### Income distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Income distribution")]

GDP does not account for [income distribution](/wiki/Income_distribution "Income distribution"). This can lead to misleading characterizations of economic well-being if the income distribution is heavily skewed toward the high end, as the poorer residents will not directly benefit from the overall level of wealth and income generated in their country (their income might decline, even as the mean [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita") rises). GDP per capita measures (like aggregate GDP measures) do not account for income distribution (and tend to overstate the average income per capita). For example, South Africa during apartheid ranked high in terms of GDP per capita, but the benefits of this immense wealth and income were not shared equally among its citizens.[[88]](#cite_note-88) The United Nations has aimed in its [Sustainable Development Goals](/wiki/Sustainable_Development_Goal_10 "Sustainable Development Goal 10"), amongst other global initiatives, to address wealth inequality.[[89]](#cite_note-89) See [income inequality metrics](/wiki/Income_inequality_metrics "Income inequality metrics") for discussion of a variety of inequality-based economic measures.[[36]](#cite_note-limitations-36) A representative measure of the living standard is the [median income](/wiki/Median_income "Median income") since it represents the [median voter](/wiki/Median_voter_theorem "Median voter theorem").[[90]](#cite_note-x744-90) The median income growth was found diverging from the GDP per capita growth in some countries.[[91]](#cite_note-10.1111_roiw.12362-91)

### Standard of living

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Standard of living")]

Main article: [Standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living")

Although a high or rising level of GDP per capita is often associated with increased economic and social progress, the opposite sometimes occurs. For example, [Jean Drèze](/wiki/Jean_Dr%C3%A8ze "Jean Drèze") and [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen") have pointed out that an increase in GDP or in GDP growth does not necessarily lead to a higher standard of living, particularly in areas such as healthcare and education.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Another important area that does not necessarily improve along with GDP is political liberty, which is most notable in China, where GDP growth is strong yet political liberties are heavily restricted.[[93]](#cite_note-93) GDP does not account for the distribution of income among the residents of a country, because GDP is merely an aggregate measure. An economy may be highly developed or growing rapidly, but also contain a wide gap between the rich and the poor in a society. These inequalities often occur on the lines of race, ethnicity, gender, religion, or other minority status within countries.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

### Capabilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Capabilities")]

In the 1980s, [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen") and [Martha Nussbaum](/wiki/Martha_Nussbaum "Martha Nussbaum") developed the [capability approach](/wiki/Capability_approach "Capability approach"), which focuses on the functional capabilities enjoyed by people within a country, rather than the aggregate GDP within a country. These capabilities consist of the functions that a person is able to achieve.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
In 1990, [Mahbub ul Haq](/wiki/Mahbub_ul_Haq "Mahbub ul Haq"), a Pakistani economist at the United Nations, introduced the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI). The HDI is a composite index of life expectancy at birth, adult literacy rate and standard of living measured as a logarithmic function of GDP, adjusted to purchasing power parity.
In 2009 Professors [Joseph Stiglitz](/wiki/Joseph_Stiglitz "Joseph Stiglitz"), [Amartya Sen](/wiki/Amartya_Sen "Amartya Sen"), and [Jean-Paul Fitoussi](/wiki/Jean-Paul_Fitoussi "Jean-Paul Fitoussi") at the [Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress](/wiki/Commission_on_the_Measurement_of_Economic_Performance_and_Social_Progress "Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress") (CMEPSP), formed by French President, [Nicolas Sarkozy](/wiki/Nicolas_Sarkozy "Nicolas Sarkozy") published a proposal to overcome the limitation of GDP economics to expand the focus to well-being economics with a well-being framework consisting of health, environment, work, physical safety, economic safety, and political freedom. This has been adopted in a number of countries as a [wellbeing economy](/wiki/Wellbeing_economy "Wellbeing economy") policy.
In 2008, the [Centre for Bhutan Studies](/wiki/Centre_for_Bhutan_Studies_and_GNH_Research "Centre for Bhutan Studies and GNH Research") began publishing the [Bhutan Gross National Happiness (GNH) Index](/wiki/Gross_National_Happiness#Bhutanese_GNH_index "Gross National Happiness"), whose contributors to happiness include physical, mental, and spiritual health; time balance; social and community vitality; cultural vitality; education; living standards; good governance; and ecological vitality.[[96]](#cite_note-96)
In 2013, the [OECD Better Life Index](/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index "OECD Better Life Index") was published by the OECD. The dimensions of the index included health, economic, workplace, income, jobs, housing, civic engagement, and [life satisfaction](/wiki/Life_satisfaction "Life satisfaction").
Since 2012, John Helliwell, [Richard Layard](/wiki/Richard_Layard,_Baron_Layard "Richard Layard, Baron Layard") and [Jeffrey Sachs](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sachs "Jeffrey Sachs") have edited an annual [World Happiness Report](/wiki/World_Happiness_Report "World Happiness Report") which reports a national measure of subjective well-being, derived from a single survey question on satisfaction with life. GDP explains some of the cross-national variation in life satisfaction, but more of it is explained by other, social variables.

### Sustainability of growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gross_domestic_product&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sustainability of growth")]

In the second decade of the 21st century the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") gave the task of creating a measurement of progress that takes “human wellbeing, sustainability and equity” into account to a group co-chaired by [Kaushik Basu](/wiki/Kaushik_Basu "Kaushik Basu") and [Nora Lustig](/wiki/Nora_Lustig "Nora Lustig"). In 2025 it published a report which argued that recent global shocks make the need for transformation more urgent. In January 2026, the UN held a conference named "Beyond GDP" attended by major economists. In February 2026, [Antonio Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres") said: "We must place true value on the environment and go beyond gross domestic product as a measure of human progress and wellbeing." The initiative followed a debate about [sustainable](/wiki/Sustainable "Sustainable") economic structures between [green growth](/wiki/Green_growth "Green growth") advocates, green [Keynesians](/wiki/Keynesians "Keynesians") and advocates of [doughnut economy](/w/index.php?title=Doughnut_economy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Doughnut economy (page does not exist)"), [Wellbeing economy](/wiki/Wellbeing_economy "Wellbeing economy"), [steady-state economics](/wiki/Steady-state_economics "Steady-state economics"), [degrowth](/wiki/Degrowth "Degrowth"), and according to a recent survey, 73% of around 800 climate policy researchers support post-growth. According to [Jason Hickel](/wiki/Jason_Hickel "Jason Hickel") this initiative will not be enough, because: “A deeper system change is required. Specifically, we need to democratise control over production, which can enable us to change what we produce and for whom. “The dominance of GDP is not an accident, it occurs because GDP measures what is valuable to capital. It is the structure of capitalism that ultimately must be overcome.”[[97]](#cite_note-97)

* [Bureau of Economic Analysis: Official United States GDP data](https://www.bea.gov/national/index.htm#gdp)
* [Historicalstatistics.org: Links to historical statistics on GDP for countries and regions](http://www.historicalstatistics.org/), maintained by the Department of Economic History at [Stockholm University](/wiki/Stockholm_University "Stockholm University")
* [Historical U.S. GDP (yearly data)](http://www.measuringworth.org/usgdp/), 1790–present, maintained by Samuel H. Williamson and Lawrence H. Officer, both professors of economics at the [University of Illinois at Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Chicago "University of Illinois at Chicago")
* [The Maddison Project](https://www.rug.nl/ggdc/historicaldevelopment/maddison/releases/maddison-project-database-2020?lang=en) of the Groningen Growth and Development Centre at the [University of Groningen](/wiki/University_of_Groningen "University of Groningen"). This project extends the work of [Angus Maddison](/wiki/Angus_Maddison "Angus Maddison") in collating all the available, credible data estimating GDP for countries around the world. This includes data for some countries for over 2,000 years and for all countries since 1950.
